text: The surgeon uses robotic scissors to carefully take down the gastropexy suture located on the patient's left side, near the greater curvature of the stomach. The band is identified and freed from surrounding adhesions and scar tissue, with the robotic camera providing a clear view of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to carefully dissect and remove the gastropexy suture from the greater curvature of the stomach, which was previously used to secure the lap band. The robotic camera provides a clear view of the anatomy, revealing adhesions and scar tissue in the area.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to carefully take down the vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach. The procedure involves meticulous dissection to remove adhesions while staying close to the stomach to prevent bleeding and avoid accessory vessels.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to carefully take down the vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring to stay close to the stomach to prevent bleeding and avoid accessory vessels. The lap band is freed to allow placement of a bougie, which is later inserted after the short gastric vessels are dissected.

The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to expose the surgical field. The lap band is removed to allow for the placement of a bougie. The short gastric vessels are taken down, and a Vazigi bougie device is inserted with the assistance of anesthesia.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to take down adhesions between the stomach and spleen, carefully separating the angle of His. The lap band remains in place, protecting the esophagus, while scar tissue is visible around the esophagus and gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon loosens the lap band and places a bougie into the stomach. The band is then removed from the stomach and placed aside. The surgeon inspects the band for any brown discoloration, which could indicate a leak or erosion. Adhesive bands are examined and divided if necessary to prevent stricture. The esophagus is circumferentially dissected to ensure clear anatomy before proceeding with the sleeve gastrectomy using a robotic stapler.

The surgeon loosens the lap band and places a bougie into the stomach. The lap band is then carefully removed from the stomach and set aside. The surgeon inspects the band for any brown discoloration, which could indicate a leak or erosion of the stomach. Adhesive bands are examined and divided to ensure there is no significant stricture.

The surgeon circumferentially dissects the area around the esophagus to ensure clear anatomy. A bougie is placed, and the robotic stapler is used to begin the sleeve gastrectomy procedure. The surgeon uses a green load for the initial staple fire, transitioning to a blue load as the stomach thickness changes. The staple line is carefully inspected after each fire to ensure proper placement and avoid interference with future stapling.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to fire the initial green load on the stomach, transitioning to a blue load as the tissue thickness changes. The staple line is carefully inspected for any interference with future stapling, and adjustments are made to the bougie placement as the stapling progresses up the stomach.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to hold the stomach flat, aligning the stapler along the bougie to perform the final staple fires. The staple line is cleared of scar tissue, ensuring the stomach is healthy and viable. The stapling process is completed at the gastroesophageal junction, with careful attention to avoid extra tension.

The surgeon cleans the crotch of the staple line before firing the stapler, ensuring the stomach is flat and aligned with the bougie. The robotic arms hold the stomach in its normal anatomical position, minimizing tension. The surgeon performs the final staple fires at the gastroesophageal junction, clearing the staple line and ensuring the stomach is viable. The stomach is separated and placed aside, while anesthesia removes the bougie.

The surgeon completes the final stapling of the stomach, ensuring the staple line closely hugs the bougie to avoid leaving excess fundus. The stomach is separated and set aside, while anesthesia removes the bougie. Observing a slight torsion of the stomach, the surgeon places a vical suture to pexy it to the peripancreatic fat, ensuring the stomach lays flat without excessive tension to maintain a smooth contour.

The surgeon observes torsion in the stomach and applies a vical suture to pexy it to the peripancreatic fat, ensuring the stomach lays flat without excessive tension. An intraoperative gastroscopy is performed to confirm the absence of torsion or leaks, and the anatomy of the sleeve is smooth. Fiber and sealant are applied robotically to the staple line, with a portion of the patient's omentum placed on top for additional hemostasis.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to suture, followed by performing an intraoperative gastroscopy to check for torsion or leaks in the sleeve anatomy. A fibrin sealant is applied robotically for hemostasis, and a portion of the patient's omentum is placed over the staple line.
